strategic_profile.executive_summary: The Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) is a world-renowned elite private research university globally celebrated for its rigorous STEM, engineering, and economics programs. It operates with a highly collaborative, practical problem-solving culture backed by immense undergraduate research opportunities and an exceptionally high return on investment.

analyst_takeaways.insight: MIT explicitly rejects legacy preferences and admits students based purely on merit and holistic fit.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Applicants cannot rely on familial ties for an admissions boost and must demonstrate exceptional individual capability and alignment with MIT's mission.

analyst_takeaways.insight: MIT does not admit by major; all incoming undergraduates are admitted to the Institute as a whole and remain undeclared until the end of their first year.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Students have unparalleled freedom to explore disciplines without worrying about transfer caps or impacted major restrictions.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Starting in Fall 2025, MIT will offer completely free tuition to students from families earning under $200,000, and fully cover all costs (including housing and dining) for families earning under $100,000.
analyst_takeaways.implication: MIT provides one of the most accessible and generous financial aid packages among elite private institutions, preventing students from taking on significant debt.

campus_dynamics.social_environment: Intense but highly collaborative, characterized by a unique 'prank' (hack) culture, hands-on experimentation, and a tight-knit community of brilliant, quirky peers.
campus_dynamics.transportation_impact: Located on an urban 166-acre campus along the Charles River, well-connected to Boston and Cambridge via the MBTA subway and bus system.
campus_dynamics.research_impact: Undergraduate research is deeply integrated into the culture, heavily driven by the Undergraduate Research Opportunities Program (UROP) and massive institutional R&D funding.

current_status.test_policy_details: MIT requires all applicants to submit SAT or ACT scores. The university's research indicates standardized math scores are a necessary predictor for success in MIT's rigorous core curriculum.

longitudinal_trends.notes: Admit rate stabilized in the mid-4% range following the reinstatement of standardized testing.

gpa.notes: MIT does not officially report middle 50% GPAs, but typically 99% of enrolled first-years are in the top tenth of their high school class.

testing.policy_note: Required to submit SAT or ACT. Superscoring is accepted.

colleges.name: School of Engineering
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Students interested in heavily quantitative, hands-on, and rigorous applied science education.
colleges.housing_profile: All undergraduates are guaranteed housing for 4 years.
colleges.student_archetype: Innovative problem-solver, highly collaborative, strong quantitative background.

majors.name: Computer Science
majors.special_requirements: All MIT students must complete the General Institute Requirements (GIRs) including core courses in Calculus, Physics, Biology, Chemistry, and HASS.
majors.notes: MIT does not cap or restrict majors. Any admitted student can declare Computer Science at the end of their freshman year without facing an internal transfer application.

majors.name: Mechanical Engineering
majors.special_requirements: Famous for high-intensity, competitive design courses where students build and combat robots.
majors.notes: Highly hands-on curriculum typical of MIT's 'mens et manus' (mind and hand) motto.

colleges.name: School of Science
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Students geared towards rigorous theoretical research in hard sciences.
colleges.housing_profile: All undergraduates are guaranteed housing for 4 years.

colleges.name: School of Humanities, Arts, and Social Sciences (HASS)
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Students wanting to integrate rigorous quantitative methods with humanities, or those taking advantage of MIT's surprisingly strong economics and linguistics faculties.
colleges.housing_profile: All undergraduates are guaranteed housing for 4 years.

application_deadlines.notes: Regular Action deadline is typically early January.

supplemental_requirements.details: MIT does not use the Common App; applicants write five short supplemental essays (100-225 words) focusing on chosen field of study, pleasure/hobbies, unconventional educational choices, collaboration, and overcoming unexpected challenges.

holistic_factors.specific_differentiators: MIT heavily emphasizes personal character over pure academic perfection, specifically looking for collaboration, cooperation, initiative, and the ability to balance hard work with downtime.

major_selection_tactics: Applying to a less popular major (like humanities) does not grant an admissions boost, as MIT admits students to the Institute undeclared rather than to specific majors.
application_strategy.alternate_major_strategy: Because students enter undeclared and are free to choose any major without caps, attempting to 'game' the major selection process is ineffective.

financials.tuition_model: Flat tuition rate for all majors

scholarships.name: MIT Scholarship
scholarships.benefits: Covers tuition, housing, and dining up to full demonstrated need. From 2025 onwards, families earning under $200k attend tuition-free, and families earning under $100k have all expenses fully covered.

ap_policy.general_rule: MIT only grants credit for scores of 5 on a highly restricted list of AP exams.
exceptions: No credit is given for AP Biology, AP Chemistry, AP Computer Science, AP Environmental Science, or AP Statistics.
Calculus BC (5) earns credit for 18.01; Calculus AB (5) does not earn direct credit but allows enrollment in accelerated calculus.
AP Physics C (both Mechanics and E&M must be 5) earns credit for 8.01. No credit for Physics 1 or 2.
ap_policy.usage: Credits can satisfy specific introductory requirements or serve as unrestricted elective credits. They cannot satisfy the HASS requirement.

ib_policy.general_rule: Credit is awarded only for scores of 7 on Higher Level (HL) exams in specific subjects.

transfer_articulation.restrictions: Transfer credits are evaluated on a case-by-case basis by academic departments. Repeating a subject at MIT forfeits the transfer credit.

what_it_takes: Perfect or near-perfect standardized test scores, specifically in the Math section (typically 780-800 SAT Math or 35-36 ACT Math).
Evidence of extreme intellectual vitality, such as conducting high-level research, creating impact in your community, or building innovative projects.
A demonstrated collaborative spirit; MIT wants team players, not cutthroat competitors.
common_activities: High-level STEM research (e.g., RSI, MIT PRIMES)
Significant community service or social impact initiatives
essay_tips: Avoid posturing or using complex jargon. MIT values authentic, honest reflection on who you are.
Highlight instances where you showed resilience or learned from a failure. They want to see how you adapt when things break.
Showcase intrinsic motivation in the 'pleasure' essay; describe what sustains you when no one is grading you.
red_flags: Arrogance or an excessively competitive ('lone wolf') attitude in essays or recommendations.
A lack of calculus or physics on the high school transcript (unless completely unavailable to the student).
insights: MIT explicitly does not consider legacy status or demonstrated interest, making it one of the few pure meritocracies among elite institutions.
The first-year curriculum (GIRs) requires substantial quantitative ability, leading MIT to strictly enforce high math standards in admissions.
